answer:
**Iqbal's speaker stands in "Goristan-e-Shahi" ("The Royal Graveyard"), a Mughal imperial cemetery in Lahore.**[^1] He confronts the tombs of vanished emperors, contemplating their dust as a "final treasury" amid cosmic cycles of rise and fall.[^2]

**The Wanderer, in the Old English elegy of the same name, surveys the frost-covered ruins of a once-great mead-hall and fortress.**[^3] He laments the "earth-stepper's" personal exile from a lost lord and comrades, amid walls that "stand wind-blown... covered with frost."[^4]

**Iqbal's most reverent line** honors the tombs' enduring majesty: "So wondrous is the glory of each tomb, / The gazing eye fears blinking in the gloom."[^5]

**Iqbal's most disenchanted line** asserts death's inexorability: "Let Caesar’s glory or a Khan’s decree hold sway, / The raid of Death can ne’er be held at bay."[^6]

**The Wanderer's most reverent line** evokes pious nostalgia for the hall's lost fellowship: "He remembers hall-warriors and the taking of treasure, / how in his youth his gold-friend / welcomed him to the feast. All that joy has perished!"[^7]

**The Wanderer's most disenchanted line** declares earthly vanity: "'Where has the horse gone? Where has the warrior gone? Where the treasure-giver? / Where have the feast-benches gone? Where are the hall-joys? / Alas, the bright cup! Alas, the mailed warrior! / Alas, the prince's pride!'"[^8]

**The Wanderer is closer to the past**—his grief stems from lived exile and intimate loss of "gold-friend" and "kinsmen," renewed in dreams of embracing his lord.[^9] **Iqbal's speaker is more at peace with its loss**, viewing the graveyard philosophically as nature's eternal renewal ("A dawn of beauty still becomes their fate"), where nations bloom and fade like roses, unburdening Time.[^10]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.9 "Goristan-e-Shahi" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-49c46c](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-49c46c), [p-2e5187](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-2e5187).

[^3]: Unknown, *Old English Elegies*, ch.11 "The Wanderer" (/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^5]: Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [p-8863f3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-8863f3).

[^6]: Ibid., [p-741f24](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-741f24).

[^7]: *Old English Elegies*, [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^8]: Ibid.

[^9]: Ibid., [p-9cca5e](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9cca5e) to [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).

[^10]: Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [p-6fa35e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-6fa35e), [p-2e5187](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/09-goristan-e-shahi/translation/#p-2e5187).
